planting.note: Plant after frost in a warm spot, or grow in a pot to overwinter indoors.

directions: Set in well-drained soil and train the semi-climbing stems onto a support. Keep warm and evenly moist, trimming to shape after flushes of bloom. Pinwheel-shaped white flowers with slender petals and purple undersides carry a strong fragrance in warm months.

planting.note: Plant in warm frost-free soil; keep contained as it can naturalize in tropical areas.

directions: Give the strong twining stems a sturdy support and prune hard to keep the plant in check. Water during dry spells and feed sparingly. Clusters of white, sweetly scented flowers open through the warm season, followed by black berries.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun; train upward for a climbing effect or grow as a shrub.

directions: Set in free-draining soil and tie the semi-woody stems to a support for height. Water in dry weather and remove old wood after flowering. Larger, sweetly scented golden-yellow flowers open in showy clusters through summer.

planting.note: Plant after frost in a sunny, sheltered position with a trellis.

directions: Guide the vigorous stems up a support and keep the roots cool and moist. Prune to shape after the main flush of flowers. Broad, creamy-white blooms are strongly perfumed and appear over a long summer season.

planting.note: Plant after frost on a warm wall where the red buds show well.

directions: Train the twining stems onto wires or a trellis and tie them in early. Water in dry weather and cut back lightly after flowering. Deep reddish buds open to fragrant white flowers, giving a two-tone effect all summer.

planting.note: Plant after frost against a warm sunny wall or pergola.

directions: Provide a strong support and tie in the vigorous twining stems as they extend. Keep the soil moist and prune after flowering to control size. This large-flowered form bears pink-flushed white blooms with an especially strong perfume all summer.

planting.note: Set out rooted plants only after all frost danger has passed and the soil is warm.

directions: Plant in fast-draining soil at the same depth as the nursery pot and tie the young canes to a sturdy trellis or wall support. Water sparingly once established and withhold nitrogen, since lean, slightly dry conditions trigger the heaviest bract color. The papery bracts, in vivid magenta-purple, peak through the warm months and repeat after each dry spell.

planting.note: Plant against a warm, sheltered wall after the last spring frost.

directions: Give this vigorous, thorny climber a strong structure and room to ramble, planting it slightly high so the crown never sits in wet soil. Prune hard after a bloom flush to keep it in bounds and to spur new flowering wood. Expect broad clusters of rose to purple bracts across the warmest months, with the display intensifying in bright, dry heat.

planting.note: Establish in warm soil after frost; it flowers readily even on young plants.

directions: Plant in gritty, well-drained soil and train the loose, arching stems onto wires or a fence. This species blooms almost continuously in bright light, so let it dry between waterings to keep bracts coming rather than lush green growth. Look for magenta to lilac bracts held against green, nearly thornless canes.

planting.note: Plant out after frost into a hot, sunny spot with sharp drainage.

directions: This natural hybrid group is the source of many warm-toned garden cultivars, so give it a substantial support and full sun. Keep feeding light and water restrained to favor bract production over foliage. Bracts range from crimson to orange and gold depending on the selection, flushing repeatedly through the warm season.

planting.note: Plant after frost into a hot spot for the richest gold tones.

directions: Plant in sharply drained soil and train onto a trellis or let it mound over a bank. Bright sun and restrained watering keep the warm color coming and the growth compact. The bracts open a clear golden-yellow and may soften toward apricot as they mature.

planting.note: Set out after frost; grows lower and mounding, ideal for containers.

directions: This variegated, semi-dwarf selection has green leaves edged in creamy gold, so give it strong light to keep the markings crisp. Grow it in a large pot or as a spilling groundcover and pinch the tips to build density. Cerise-pink bracts stand out against the two-tone foliage through the warm months.

planting.note: Plant after frost; compact and nearly thornless, good near walkways.

directions: Grow this low, bushy, near-thornless selection as a shrub, hedge, or container plant where its small size is an asset. Trim lightly to keep it rounded and full, and let it dry between waterings. Pure white bracts appear almost year-round in frost-free climates and take on a faint pink cast in cooler spells.

planting.note: Plant after frost; naturally low and mounding for beds and pots.

directions: This dwarf, spreading selection works well as a groundcover, in containers, or trained as a low informal hedge. Keep it in full sun and on the dry side to encourage bloom. The bracts open golden-orange and gradually shift to soft pink, so the plant shows two tones at once.

planting.note: Plant after frost; shrubby habit suits foundations and large pots.

directions: Use this compact, mounding red as a shrub or container specimen rather than a tall climber. Prune to shape after each bloom flush and grow it hard in full sun for the best color. Rich red bracts cover the bushy growth heavily through the warm season.

planting.note: Plant after frost; upright, non-vining habit needs no trellis.

directions: Unlike typical climbers, this selection grows as a stiff, upright shrub with short, congested stems, so plant it as a freestanding accent. Give it full sun and lean, dry conditions and skip the trellis entirely. Clusters of hot pink to magenta bracts sit at the branch tips like small torches through the warm months.

planting.note: Plant after frost; blooms freely even as a young plant.

directions: Train this free-flowering purple onto a fence or arbor and give it plenty of heat and sun. It sets bracts almost continuously, so keep watering restrained to avoid soft, bloomless growth. The saturated deep-purple bracts hold well and contrast sharply with the green foliage.

planting.note: Plant after frost; noted for unusually large bracts.

directions: Give this vigorous climber a large, strong support to show off its oversized bracts. Grow it lean and dry in full sun and prune after each flush to keep it manageable. The big bracts open reddish and mature toward magenta-purple, creating a layered display.

planting.note: Plant after frost; the original crimson buttiana hybrid.

directions: This historic hybrid is a strong climber that rewards a large trellis or wall and full sun. Keep feeding light and water minimal once established to favor bract color. Crimson to rose-red bracts appear in generous flushes across the warm months.

planting.note: Plant after frost into a hot, bright site for glowing color.

directions: Train this warm-toned climber where morning or full sun can light up its bracts. Keep it lean and dry to intensify the color and limit leafy growth. The bracts glow golden-orange to apricot, with the effect strongest in bright, hot weather.

planting.note: Plant after frost; vigorous and very free-flowering.

directions: This strong grower produces enormous clusters of bracts, so give it a large support and full sun. Keep it lean and slightly dry to maximize the display. Bright pink to magenta bracts appear in massive, showy flushes through the warm season.

planting.note: Plant after frost; prized for its bicolor bracts.

directions: Train this moderate climber where its two-tone bracts can be seen up close. Full sun and restrained watering keep the color true and the growth in check. Bracts open white with pink edges and deepen toward rose as they age, so the plant shows a soft blend.

planting.note: Plant after frost; double bracts hold color longer than singles.

directions: This double-bracted red carries clustered, rosette-like bracts that persist well and shed less than single forms. Grow it in full sun with lean soil and sparing water. Deep red doubles appear in dense heads through the warm months, aging to a slightly muted tone.

planting.note: Plant after frost; blends warm tones on the same plant.

directions: Give this colorful climber a sunny wall so its shifting tones stand out. Keep it lean and dry to favor bracts over foliage. The bracts open gold to coral and mature to pink, so a single plant carries a festival of warm colors at once.

planting.note: Plant after frost; double pink bracts are long-lasting.

directions: This double-flowered pink holds its clustered bracts for weeks and drops less litter than single types. Grow it in full sun with sharp drainage and modest water. Soft pink double bracts form rosette-like heads across the warm season.

planting.note: Plant after frost; clean white bracts brighten shaded corners.

directions: Train this white-bracted climber on a wall or arbor where the pale bracts contrast with dark green leaves. Full sun and lean, dry conditions keep it flowering. Crisp white bracts, sometimes veined faintly green, appear in flushes through the warm months and can pick up a pink blush in cool weather.

planting.note: Plant after frost; a vigorous purple for large structures.

directions: This strong-growing purple covers walls, pergolas, and large fences quickly. Give it full sun and prune hard after flushes to keep it dense and flowering. Bluish-purple bracts appear in heavy sprays across the warm season.

planting.note: Plant after frost; famous for carrying two bract colors at once.

directions: This vigorous climber often bears white and purple bracts on the same plant, so site it where the contrast shows. Keep it in full sun with lean soil and sparing water. Expect a striking mix of white and magenta-purple bracts through the warm months.

planting.note: Plant after frost; a clear yellow-gold climber.

directions: Grow this golden-yellow selection in full sun where the warm color reads best. Keep it lean and dry to encourage bract set. Bright yellow bracts flush repeatedly and may age to a soft cream or apricot in strong heat.

planting.note: Plant after frost; warm gold-orange bracts on a strong vine.

directions: Train this warm-toned climber onto a sunny wall or fence for the best color. Water sparingly once established and prune after each flush. Golden-orange bracts appear in generous flushes and soften toward pink as they age.

planting.note: Plant after frost; naturally dwarf and pot-friendly.

directions: Part of the compact Bambino group, this dwarf stays small enough for patio pots and low borders without heavy pruning. Grow it in full sun with sharp drainage and light watering. Warm orange to coral bracts cover the tidy plant through the warm season.

planting.note: Plant after frost; bicolor bracts on a compact plant.

directions: This compact selection works well in containers and low hedges where its two-tone bracts show up close. Keep it in full sun and let it dry between waterings. Bracts open white and are edged and washed with pink, giving a soft, layered effect.

planting.note: Plant after frost; intense red bracts on a free bloomer.

directions: Give this fiery selection a sunny wall or trellis and lean, well-drained soil. Water sparingly once rooted in to keep the heavy bloom coming. Vivid fire-red bracts flush repeatedly and stay saturated through the warm season.

planting.note: Plant after frost; soft two-tone pink and white bracts.

directions: Train this gentle-colored climber where its pale bracts can be admired at close range. Grow it in full sun with restrained water to keep it blooming. Bracts open white with pink margins and deepen toward rose, evoking a cherry-blossom blend.

planting.note: Plant after frost; a dependable purple for containers and walls.

directions: This adaptable purple grows well in the ground or in large pots and flowers reliably when young. Give it full sun, sharp drainage, and modest water. Rich magenta-purple bracts appear in dense flushes through the warm months.

common_name: Arizona Dark Red Black-eyed Susan Vine

planting.note: Sow indoors ahead of the last frost for early flowering.

directions: Start seed shallowly in warm soil and provide bright light as seedlings develop. The deep brick-red petals set against a near-black eye look best trailing from a basket or twining up a low trellis. Water steadily in heat and remove spent blooms to prolong the display.

common_name: Susie Orange with Eye Clock Vine

planting.note: Raise transplants indoors and set out when the soil has warmed.

directions: Cover seed with about a quarter inch of warm, moist mix and keep it lit until sprouting. This classic strain carries bright orange flowers with a dark chocolate throat on quick-climbing stems. Guide it onto a fence or obelisk and keep it watered for bloom from early summer to frost.

common_name: Susie White with Eye Clock Vine

planting.note: Start indoors in spring and transplant once frost risk has ended.

directions: Sow shallowly in warm soil and hold steady moisture until seedlings appear. Creamy white petals framing a dark eye give a cooler look that pairs well with hot-colored companions. Train the twining shoots up a trellis and pinch back early to build a fuller plant.

common_name: Susie Yellow with Eye Clock Vine

planting.note: Grow transplants indoors and plant out after the last frost.

directions: Set seed a quarter inch deep in warm mix and keep bright and damp until it sprouts. Sunny yellow blooms with a dark center light up a support all season. Provide string or mesh to climb and water regularly so flowering does not stall in midsummer heat.

planting.note: Start indoors before the last frost for a jump on the season.

directions: Sow into warm, moist soil at shallow depth and keep evenly damp until germination. This blend offers soft apricot, terracotta, and cream tones that mingle as the vine climbs. Let it trail from a basket or scale a trellis, and deadhead now and then to keep it tidy.

planting.note: Begin indoors in spring and transplant after nights turn mild.

directions: Press seed lightly into warm mix and keep it moist and bright until sprouts show. Flowers open in muted shades of rose, apricot, ivory, and dusky red for a soft, romantic effect. Grow it up a support or spill it from a container and feed monthly for steady bloom.

planting.note: Sow indoors ahead of the last frost or buy started plants.

directions: Start seed shallowly in warm soil and keep it evenly moist during germination. Bright lemon-yellow flowers with a dark eye cover this vigorous, self-branching selection. Give it a trellis or let it cascade, and water consistently through summer heat for nonstop bloom.

planting.note: Raise indoors before the last frost and transplant once warm.

directions: Cover seed with about a quarter inch of warm mix and keep it bright and damp until sprouting. Deep orange petals around a dark throat bloom heavily on tidy, mounding-to-climbing stems. Provide support or a hanging basket and keep soil moist for flowers all season.

common_name: Tangerine Slice A-Peel Black-eyed Susan Vine

planting.note: Start indoors in spring or set out purchased plants after frost.

directions: Sow seed shallowly in warm soil and hold steady moisture until it germinates. This eye-catching selection shows bicolor petals of orange and deep red radiating from a dark center. Train it up a support or trail it from a pot and deadhead lightly to keep it blooming.

planting.note: Sow indoors before the last frost for early, large blooms.

directions: Set seed a quarter inch deep in warm mix and keep it moist and lit until sprouts emerge. This strain is noted for oversized orange flowers with a bold dark eye on strong climbing stems. Guide it up a trellis or arch and water regularly to sustain the large blooms.

common_name: Sunny Susy Red Orange Clock Vine

planting.note: Begin indoors in spring and plant out after the last frost.

directions: Press seed shallowly into warm, damp soil and keep bright until germination. Two-toned petals of red and orange surround a dark throat for a warm, glowing effect. Provide netting or a fence to climb and keep evenly watered for continuous flowering.

planting.note: Begin seed indoors in late winter and transplant out once nights stay reliably warm.

directions: Press seeds edgewise into warm moist mix and be patient, as they may take three weeks to sprout. Train the fast, tendril-climbing stems up a tall support in full sun with steady moisture and occasional feeding. This form carries the same creamy-green cups but they mature to pure ivory-white rather than purple, blooming from late summer to frost.

planting.note: Start indoors well ahead of the last frost and move out once soil is warm.

directions: Sow on the surface of a warm seed tray and keep humid until the tiny seedlings appear. This selection is prized for heavy, uniform flowering and looks best cascading from a basket or scrambling up a warm trellis with light afternoon shade. Pendant pink-and-black bells hang along the stems from early summer through autumn.

planting.note: Start seed under cover in spring and plant out when frost has passed.

directions: Sow shallowly in warmth and grow on until the frost-free planting window opens. Train the light, tendril-gripping stems up a warm wall or obelisk in full sun with free-draining soil. This variety blooms in clear golden-yellow tubes from summer into autumn, extending its display where mild winters allow it to persist.

planting.note: Germinate indoors in spring and transplant outdoors after the last frost.

directions: Cover the seed lightly and keep evenly moist and warm until it sprouts. Set young plants against a sunny fence or trellis where the coiling tendrils can grip, and water regularly through the growing season. The tubular flowers of this form open in shades of carmine-rose to pink from early summer until cut back by frost.

planting.note: Sow indoors in early spring and plant out once frosts have finished.

directions: Start the fine seed in warmth and grow the seedlings on until settled weather arrives. This seed strain produces a lively mix of orange, red, pink, and yellow tubular flowers on vigorous tendril-climbing stems. Give it full sun and a sheltered support, and it will bloom from midsummer until frost, often self-sowing in mild gardens.

planting.note: Raise under glass in spring and plant out after the danger of frost.

directions: Sow the small seed shallowly in warmth and grow the seedlings on strongly before planting out. This less common species climbs by tendrils and needs a warm, sheltered sunny position with free-draining soil. It bears slender, elongated yellow tubular flowers over a long summer season, appealing to collectors of unusual climbers.

planting.note: Set rhizomes out in early spring once the soil can be worked.

directions: Plant rhizomes horizontally about two inches deep with the buds pointing up, and give the vigorous bines a sturdy trellis, string, or arbor to climb. Water steadily through the first season while the crown establishes, then cut spent bines to the ground each winter. Papery green cones ripen in late summer and feel light and springy when ready.

planting.note: Plant rooted divisions in spring against a support in bright light.

directions: Grow this chartreuse-leaved form where it gets morning sun but afternoon shade, which keeps the golden foliage bright without scorching. Train the twining bines up a fence or pergola and mulch the base to hold moisture. Foliage color is the main show, with soft green cones following in late summer.

planting.note: Set out divisions in spring where the compact bines can climb a small support.

directions: This shorter golden selection suits containers and low trellises, holding brighter leaf color than older gold forms with less scorching. Give it a moist but well-drained root run and light afternoon shade in hot regions. Enjoy the glowing foliage through summer and the light-green cones that follow.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a spot with steady light for best variegation.

directions: Prized for green leaves splashed and speckled with cream, this form climbs readily up strings or an arbor. Remove any all-green shoots that appear so the variegation stays dominant. Cut the bines back hard in late fall and expect fresh marbled growth each spring.

planting.note: Plant potted divisions in spring on a trellis or large container obelisk.

directions: A bright golden ornamental hop that resists the leaf scorch common to older gold forms, making it easy in more open positions. Guide the twining bines up a support early since they climb fast in warm weather. Grown chiefly for its glowing foliage, with modest green cones as a bonus.

planting.note: Sow seed outdoors after the last frost when the soil has warmed.

directions: Sow seed about half an inch deep after frost, or start a few weeks early indoors, since this annual grows explosively once heat arrives. Provide strong netting or twine because the rough bines climb many feet in a single season. Note that it can self-sow aggressively and is considered invasive in some regions, so remove seed heads if spread is a concern.

planting.note: Start seed indoors before the last frost and set out after warm weather settles.

directions: This annual form carries leaves boldly streaked in white, giving fast summer screening with a splash of color. Start seed indoors for a head start, then transplant against netting or a trellis once nights stay warm. Grow it for the foliage effect and pinch back seed heads to limit unwanted self-sowing.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes in spring in a sunny, well-drained bed.

directions: This wild western variety tolerates heat and drier soils better than many garden hops, thriving in mountain and high-desert gardens. Set rhizomes a couple inches deep and give the vigorous bines a tall support. Once established it needs little extra water, producing aromatic cones in late summer.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes in spring at the base of a tall vertical support.

directions: A dependable, vigorous hop that climbs quickly and covers arbors or tall strings by midsummer. Set rhizomes two inches deep, train the strongest bines up the support, and keep the soil evenly moist through the growing season. Fragrant cones firm up and lighten in late summer when they are ready to pick.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes in spring where bines can climb tall twine.

directions: An upright, moderately vigorous hop that trains neatly up strings or wire in the home garden. Plant rhizomes shallowly and feed with a balanced fertilizer as the bines lengthen in early summer. Aromatic cones develop a papery feel and pale color by late season.

planting.note: Set rhizomes out in spring against a strong, tall trellis.

directions: A rugged, high-yielding hop with especially vigorous bines that need a sturdy support at least fifteen feet tall. Bury rhizomes horizontally and thin to the two or three strongest shoots per crown for cleaner growth. Large resinous cones ripen densely in late summer.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes in spring where the heavy bines have room to climb.

directions: A powerful, sprawling hop that produces abundant leafy cover and large cones on very long bines. Give it a tall, well-anchored support and keep it watered during dry spells. Cones mature heavy and aromatic toward the end of summer.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes in spring in full sun with good drainage.

directions: A hardy, disease-resistant hop that is forgiving for beginners and covers a support densely. Set rhizomes a couple inches deep, mulch well, and train the leading bines upward as they emerge. Compact firm cones ripen reliably in late summer.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes in spring beside a tall, secure trellis.

directions: A robust and healthy hop that climbs strongly and resists common mildews in the garden. Plant rhizomes shallowly and provide steady moisture as the bines race upward in early summer. Plump cones firm up and grow papery by late season.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes in spring on a sunny fence or trellis.

directions: A classic English hop of moderate vigor that suits smaller gardens and arbors well. Bury the rhizome, guide two or three bines up strings, and keep the root zone cool with mulch. Soft aromatic cones ripen in late summer.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes in spring in a warm, sheltered, sunny spot.

directions: A refined traditional English hop that grows with gentle vigor and appreciates a sheltered, sunny site. Set rhizomes shallowly and train the bines up twine, thinning weak shoots. Fragrant, delicate cones develop their papery texture toward the end of summer.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes in spring where bines can climb tall strings.

directions: A vigorous, adaptable hop derived from Fuggle that performs well across many climates. Plant rhizomes horizontally and keep them evenly moist during establishment. Soft, aromatic cones lighten and feel springy when ready in late summer.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes in spring in fertile, well-drained soil.

directions: A classic German aroma hop of moderate vigor that fits well on a home trellis. Bury the rhizome a couple inches deep and provide consistent moisture and feeding through summer. Delicate cones ripen with a papery feel in late season.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes in spring in a sunny bed with rich soil.

directions: A prized Czech aroma hop with slender bines and a gentle growth habit suited to smaller supports. Set rhizomes shallowly, keep them watered, and train the shoots as they rise. Fine, fragrant cones firm up toward the end of summer.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes in spring in fertile, moisture-retentive soil.

directions: A traditional German aroma hop of modest vigor that trains easily up garden twine. Bury the rhizome, mulch to keep roots cool, and thin to the strongest bines. Aromatic cones develop their papery texture in late summer.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes in spring in full sun with good air flow.

directions: One of the oldest American hops, hardy and productive with dependable, vigorous growth. Set rhizomes horizontally and give the bines a tall support and open air to limit mildew. Firm cones ripen abundantly in late summer.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes in spring beside a tall support.

directions: A reliable, high-yielding hop with strong bines that fill a trellis quickly. Plant rhizomes shallowly, keep the soil moist during establishment, and feed as growth accelerates. Plump cones ripen heavy and aromatic by late summer.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes in spring in a sunny, sheltered position.

directions: An American aroma hop of moderate vigor with a tidy habit good for home arbors. Bury the rhizome a couple inches deep and train two or three bines upward. Delicate, fragrant cones firm up and lighten in late summer.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes in spring in rich, well-drained soil.

directions: A Saaz-type American hop with good vigor and easy garden performance. Set rhizomes shallowly, keep them watered, and give the bines a tall string to climb. Aromatic cones develop a papery feel toward the end of summer.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes in spring in full sun with steady moisture.

directions: A mild American aroma hop with moderate, manageable growth that suits home trellises. Bury the rhizome, mulch to conserve moisture, and thin to the strongest shoots. Soft, fragrant cones ripen in late summer.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes in spring in a warm, sunny bed.

directions: A Hallertau-style American aroma hop of gentle vigor, easy to keep on a smaller support. Set the rhizome shallowly and guide the bines up twine as they emerge. Delicate cones firm up and lighten by late summer.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes in spring at the foot of a tall trellis.

directions: A dependable dual-purpose hop with sturdy, vigorous bines and good disease resistance. Plant rhizomes horizontally and keep the soil evenly moist as the bines climb. Firm cones ripen in late summer with a papery feel.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes in spring in fertile, well-drained soil.

directions: A tidy German hop of moderate vigor and good mildew resistance, well suited to garden supports. Bury the rhizome a couple inches deep and train the bines upward early. Aromatic cones develop their papery texture in late summer.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes in spring beside a tall, strong support.

directions: A vigorous heritage hop with strong bines that quickly cover a fence or arbor. Set rhizomes shallowly, keep them watered, and thin to a few leading shoots for cleaner growth. Resinous cones ripen heavy in late summer.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes in spring in full sun with good drainage.

directions: A vigorous American hop with hardy, quick-growing bines that fill a support readily. Plant rhizomes horizontally and provide consistent moisture and a tall trellis. Aromatic cones firm up and lighten toward the end of summer.

planting.note: Set out young plants after the last hard frost against a sturdy support.

directions: Sow cleaned seed shallowly after a period of cold, moist chilling, as fresh seed germinates unevenly over several weeks. Give the twining stems a fence, arbor, or trellis to climb and prune hard after flowering to keep this vigorous vine in bounds. Look for fragrant, spice-scented maroon-purple flowers in mid-spring, sometimes followed by sausage-shaped violet pods.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring once frost danger passes.

directions: Propagate this white-flowered selection from semi-ripe cuttings or layered stems, since seed will not come true. Train the new growth onto a strong frame and thin congested stems in late spring. Expect softly fragrant, greenish-white to cream blossoms in spring against fresh palmate foliage.

planting.note: Set out rooted cuttings after the last frost near a support.

directions: Increase this cultivar from cuttings or ground layers rather than seed to keep its pale coloring. Provide an arbor or wires and cut back overlong shoots after bloom to encourage tidy regrowth. The nodding cream-white flowers carry chocolate-purple centers and a light spicy scent in spring.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring where afternoon shade protects the marked leaves.

directions: Root cuttings or layer stems to preserve the cream-and-pink flecked leaves, which do not pass through seed. Give it a trellis in a spot with some midday shade so the pale variegation does not scorch. Slightly fragrant pinkish-purple flowers appear in spring among the mottled foliage.

planting.note: Set out young plants after frost near a fence or arbor.

directions: Sow chilled seed shallowly and be patient, as germination straggles over several weeks. This species carries leaves of three wavy-edged leaflets and needs sturdy support for its twining stems. Purple spring flowers may be followed by fat lavender pods where two seedlings cross-pollinate.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring against a climbing frame.

directions: Propagate this natural cross between the five-leaf and three-leaf species from cuttings or layers to keep its blend of traits. Train it up a robust support and shear back excess growth after flowering. Its foliage shows an intermediate leaflet count and it bears purple spring flowers.

planting.note: Set out plants after frost in a sheltered position with support.

directions: Sow cold-treated seed shallowly and grow the seedlings on before setting out, as this species prefers a milder, sheltered site. Provide a trellis and mulch the roots to buffer winter cold. It produces long, slender racemes of soft purple-pink flowers in spring.

planting.note: Plant after frost in dappled shade with roots kept cool.

directions: Sow seed shallowly after cold, moist chilling and expect slow, staggered germination. This twining vine likes a cool, shaded root run and a support to climb, and most plants are single-sexed so grow more than one for fruit. Fragrant pale pink flowers open in late spring, giving way to hanging strings of scarlet berries on female plants.

planting.note: Set out rooted plants in spring in partial shade with a support.

directions: Grow this self-fertile selection from cuttings or purchased plants so it holds its fruiting habit and does not need a separate pollinator. Train it onto wires or a trellis in cool, moist soil and shelter it from harsh afternoon sun. Pale flowers in spring are followed by clusters of bright red, tart berries on a single plant.

planting.note: Plant after frost in light shade with a cool root zone.

directions: Sow chilled seed shallowly and grow seedlings on in shade, since this species resents dry, exposed sites. Give it a shaded wall or tree to scramble through and plant male and female forms for the ornamental fruit. Deep crimson-red flowers hang from the stems in late spring, followed on female vines by long red berry spikes.

planting.note: Set out plants after frost in partial shade with support.

directions: Sow cold-stratified seed shallowly and keep seedlings shaded and evenly moist. Provide a trellis or shrub to twine through in cool, humus-rich soil. Small orange to salmon flowers appear in spring, and female plants ripen strings of red fruit.

planting.note: Plant after frost in a sheltered, lightly shaded spot.

directions: Sow chilled seed shallowly and grow the tender young plants under shelter until established. Give this Himalayan species a cool, shaded support and protect it from cold winds. It bears comparatively large white to pale pink flowers in spring, followed by red berry clusters.

planting.note: Set out plants after frost in a sheltered site with support.

directions: Sow cold-treated seed shallowly and raise seedlings in a protected, shaded frame. This near-evergreen twiner suits a mild garden wall or pergola in humus-rich soil. Small yellow to orange flowers open through summer and autumn, followed by red berries on female plants.

planting.note: Plant after frost in light shade against a support.

directions: Sow stratified seed shallowly and keep young plants cool and shaded while they establish. Grow it on a trellis where the distinctive winged and bloomy young stems can be admired. Pale greenish-white flowers open in early summer, followed by red fruit spikes on female vines.

planting.note: Set out potted plants after frost danger passes so roots establish in warm soil.

directions: Plant against a sturdy trellis or wall support with the crown level with the surrounding soil, then tie the young stems until they begin twining on their own. Water regularly through the first summer and mulch to keep roots cool. Expect fragrant white pinwheel flowers to open across late spring and early summer.

planting.note: Plant after frost as a dense groundcover or low climber in warm-region gardens.

directions: Space plants closely for a matting groundcover or train single plants up a support to climb. Keep evenly moist while establishing, then shear or mow overgrown mats in late winter to renew dense growth. Small creamy-yellow fragrant flowers appear sparsely in late spring on this foliage-driven species.

planting.note: Reputedly more cold-tolerant, so plant in spring even at the cool edge of its range.

directions: Plant near a wall or fence and guide the twining stems onto the support during the first season. This selection is prized for slightly better cold hardiness and heavy fragrance, so shelter it from harsh winter wind. Clusters of white star-shaped blooms perfume the garden in late spring.

planting.note: Set out in spring in a spot with some afternoon shade to protect the cream-edged leaves.

directions: Grow on a trellis or as a spilling container plant where the white-margined green foliage can be admired. Give it moderate water and shelter from midday sun to keep the variegation crisp. Fragrant white flowers open in late spring above the marbled leaves.

planting.note: Plant after frost where new growth can show its pink and white tints.

directions: Train onto a support or let it cascade from a raised bed where the pink-flushed new leaves stand out against older green and cream foliage. Keep soil evenly moist and prune lightly after flowering to shape. White fragrant blooms appear in late spring and early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sheltered, sunny spot to encourage the yellow-toned flowers.

directions: Grow against a warm wall or on an obelisk and tie in stems until they twine freely. This selection is grown for soft butter-yellow flowers that fade paler with age, so give it full sun for best color. Fragrant blooms open through late spring into summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun where the pink-blushed flowers can be enjoyed up close.

directions: Set against a trellis or pergola post and secure young stems until they climb on their own. Prized for flowers that open with a rosy-pink flush before fading, it blooms best in full sun with steady moisture. Fragrant flowers appear in late spring and repeat lightly into summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a bright groundcover or low climber in warm regions.

directions: Use as a glossy groundcover or train up a low support, spacing plants to knit together over a season or two. New growth emerges bright golden-green, aging to deep green, so a little sun brings out the color. Keep evenly watered and trim mats in late winter to keep them dense.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a durable evergreen groundcover in the warm South.

directions: Space plants across the bed for a fine-textured, low mat that tolerates heat and light foot traffic once established. Keep watered through the first summer, then mow or shear in late winter to renew clean growth. This foliage groundcover flowers only sparsely, so grow it for its dense evergreen carpet.

planting.note: Plant in spring in bright light to bring out the pink and white new growth.

directions: Grow as a colorful groundcover or short climber where the emerging pink shoots and cream-splashed leaves are visible. Provide some sun for strong coloring but shelter from harsh afternoon heat. Keep evenly moist and trim to shape after the flush of spring growth.

planting.note: Plant in spring where its distinctive narrow twisted leaves can be seen.

directions: Train onto a trellis or wall, tying in the young stems until they twine on their own. This selection has slender, wavy leaves that give a finer texture than the common form. Fragrant white pinwheel flowers open in late spring above the distinctive foliage.

planting.note: Set young plants out in spring after frost, giving them a fence or trellis to twine on.

directions: Nick or pour near-boiling water over the hard legume seed and soak overnight before sowing about a quarter inch deep in a gritty free-draining mix. Once established this twiner shrugs off drought and needs only occasional deep watering; prune lightly after flowering to keep it tidy. Sprays of pea-shaped violet flowers cover the plant from late winter into spring.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring against a strong support this vigorous form can climb.

directions: Set the potted plant at its original depth in well-drained soil and tie the leading stems onto a trellis until they grip on their own. This robust selection flowers heavily and tolerates dry spells, so water deeply but infrequently and shear back after bloom to renew growth. Expect a dense curtain of deep purple flowers through late winter and early spring.

planting.note: Plant this compact non-climbing form in spring in beds, borders, or containers.

directions: Plant this tidy mounding selection at soil level in a sunny, sharply drained spot where it needs no trellis. Water to establish, then let it dry between soakings and trim lightly after flowering to hold its rounded shape. Small purple pea flowers gather along the stems from late winter into spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring on banks or as a spreading groundcover; it will scramble but not climb high.

directions: Set plants a few feet apart on a slope or open bed at their nursery depth and mulch to suppress weeds while they knit together. This low sprawling form is drought tolerant once rooted and can be sheared to keep it dense. A haze of purple flowers runs over the foliage in late winter and spring.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring beside a trellis or arch for the twining stems.

directions: Plant at the same depth it grew in the pot and guide the young stems onto a support. This soft-pink flowered form wants full sun and fast-draining soil, with only occasional deep watering once settled. Slender sprays of rose-pink pea flowers open across late winter and early spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring against a fence or trellis so the twining stems can climb.

directions: Set the plant level with the surrounding soil in a sunny, well-drained position and train the stems upward until they twine on their own. Give it a lean site and infrequent deep watering, then trim after flowering to keep growth compact. Pure white pea flowers wreath the vine from late winter through spring.

planting.note: Plant rooted stock in spring with a trellis to carry the twining growth.

directions: Plant at nursery depth in gritty free-draining soil and tie in the first shoots until they take hold of a support. This clean white-flowered selection is drought hardy once established and benefits from a light prune after bloom. Crisp white flower sprays cover the plant in late winter and early spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring against a trellis; the loose spreading stems climb readily.

directions: Position the plant at soil level in a sunny well-drained bed and let its rambling shoots find a fence or support. It tolerates dry conditions once rooted, so soak deeply now and then rather than watering little and often. Fragrant near-white flowers with a faint blush appear generously through late winter and spring.

planting.note: Plant this shrubbier form in spring as a low mound in beds or containers.

directions: Set the plant at its existing depth in free-draining soil where its bushy habit needs little or no support. Water to establish then ease off, letting the soil dry between deep soakings, and clip after bloom to keep it full. Rich violet-blue pea flowers crowd the stems in late winter and spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring against a sturdy trellis or use as a spreading groundcover.

directions: Plant at nursery depth and either train the stems up a support or let them spread over a bank as a dense mat. This free-flowering selection is drought tolerant once settled and responds well to a hard prune after flowering. Masses of deep purple flowers blanket the plant in late winter and early spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring on a strong pergola or fence; this species climbs vigorously.

directions: Soak the hard seed in hot water overnight and sow a quarter inch deep in a gritty mix kept warm and just moist. This western Australian climber is fast and needs a solid support; water it through the first summer, then treat it as drought tolerant. Airy sprays of three-part purple-blue flowers hang from the vine in late winter and spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring with a trellis; the twining stems climb steadily.

directions: Scarify or hot-water treat the seed, then sow shallowly in a free-draining mix and keep it warm until sprouts appear. Noted for its broad leaves, this species handles dry ground once established and appreciates a light prune after bloom. Purple pea flowers open along the stems in late winter and early spring.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring on a warm trellis or archway.

directions: Plant at the depth it grew in the pot in rich, well-drained soil and tie the stems to a support until they twine. Keep it evenly watered through the growing season and protect it from frost; feed in spring for the strongest flush. Soft pink trumpets with a deeper rose throat cover the vine from late spring into autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring against a warm wall or pergola in a sheltered spot.

directions: Set the plant at soil level in fertile, free-draining ground and guide the young shoots onto their support. Water regularly during warm weather, mulch the roots, and shelter the plant from frost for reliable growth. Clean white trumpet flowers appear in flushes from late spring through summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring on a trellis in a warm, protected position.

directions: Plant at nursery depth in rich well-drained soil and train the stems up a support as they lengthen. This selection carries pure white flowers with a pale throat, so keep it evenly moist and frost-free for the longest display. Slender white trumpets open in waves from late spring into autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring where its golden-marked foliage catches the light.

directions: Plant at soil level in well-drained ground and give it a trellis for the twining stems to climb. Prized for glossy leaves splashed with gold, it grows a little less rampantly than the plain species and wants steady moisture and frost protection. Pink trumpet flowers with rosy throats appear against the variegated foliage through the warm months.

planting.note: Plant in spring on a strong support in a warm sheltered aspect.

directions: Plant at its original depth in fertile, well-drained soil and secure the leading stems to a trellis. This large-flowered form wants regular water in the growing season and shelter from frost to bloom freely. Big pink trumpets with a deep crimson throat open from late spring through summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring where the cream-edged leaves brighten a shaded fence.

directions: Set the plant at soil level in rich free-draining ground and train it onto a support. Its leaves are edged in cream, so give it bright light with some afternoon shade in hot climates, steady moisture, and protection from frost. Pale pink trumpet flowers stand out against the variegated foliage in the warm season.

planting.note: Plant in spring on a warm pergola or fence for the twining stems.

directions: Plant at nursery depth in fertile, well-drained soil and tie the stems in until they climb on their own. This vigorous pink form flowers heavily, so water through dry spells, feed in spring, and shelter it from frost. Large deep-throated pink trumpets appear in generous flushes from late spring into autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring on a large pergola or fence; this species is very vigorous.

directions: Sow seed a quarter inch deep in a warm moist mix, or plant established stock in spring against a strong support. This fast, hardy climber tolerates a range of soils and some dryness once rooted, and can be pruned hard to keep it in bounds. Clusters of small cream trumpets flecked with maroon throats appear in spring.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring on a robust pergola or fence.

directions: Plant at its nursery depth against a sturdy support and tie in the vigorous leading stems. This selection is grown for its warm color and takes most soils, needing only occasional deep watering once established. Sprays of golden-yellow trumpet flowers cover the vine in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring on a large support where the twining stems can spread.

directions: Set the plant at soil level against a strong fence or arch and guide the fast stems onto it. This white-flowered form is tough and adaptable, wanting well-drained soil and only occasional deep watering once rooted. Clusters of small pure white trumpet flowers open across the vine in spring.

planting.note: Set plants out against a warm wall or trellis once all danger of frost has passed.

directions: Plant at the base of a sturdy trellis or wires and tie in the whippy new stems as they lengthen. Give it a sheltered, sunny spot and cut back hard in early spring to keep the tangle of growth in bounds. Loose clusters of pale blue-white star flowers open from midsummer until the first frosts.

planting.note: Plant out after the last frost where the roots can stay warm and sheltered.

directions: Train this pure-white selection onto wires or an obelisk and weave the flexible shoots through the supports early in the season. Prune in spring to remove weak stems and shorten the framework, since flowers form on new growth. Fragrant white stars with golden centers appear in generous trusses from summer into autumn.

planting.note: Plant after frost in a warm, bright position that brings out the leaf coloring.

directions: Give this cream-splashed form full sun so the leaf markings stay crisp, and tie the slender stems to a support as they grow. It is a little less vigorous than the plain green vine, so prune lightly in spring just to shape it. Watch for pale blue star flowers scattered over the mottled foliage through summer and early fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring against a warm wall where the roots are sheltered from cold.

directions: This vigorous scrambler has no twining tendrils, so tie the main stems to horizontal wires as they extend. Plant in well-drained soil against a sheltered wall and cut back the previous year's growth in spring to keep it tidy. Large heads of fragrant lilac-blue flowers with yellow eyes bloom from early summer well into autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring on a warm, sheltered wall for the longest flowering display.

directions: This free-flowering, hardier selection needs its main stems tied to a framework of wires since it cannot cling on its own. Plant in fertile, well-drained soil and shorten side shoots in spring to a permanent framework. It carries a long succession of rich purple-blue flowers from early summer until the frosts, longer than most forms.

planting.note: Plant only when nights are reliably warm, as it will not tolerate any frost.

directions: Grow this tropical twiner up a trellis or into a shrub in a warm, sheltered spot, or as a container plant that overwinters indoors. Keep it evenly moist through the growing season and prune after flowering to control its size. Nodding clusters of starry lavender-blue flowers are followed by small red berries that are poisonous and best kept from children.

planting.note: Plant out in warm weather; protect or bring under cover where frost threatens.

directions: This robust vine needs a large pergola or wall and its stems tied in, as small prickles help it scramble but it will not self-cling securely. Give it rich, moist soil and a warm position, cutting it back hard in spring to renew flowering wood. Broad clusters of large lilac-blue flowers up to two inches across appear through summer and autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist ground near a hedge or fence it can scramble through.

directions: This vigorous native scrambler roots easily from cuttings and threads its lax stems up through nearby shrubs and fences. It thrives in damp soil and part shade and can spread aggressively, so plant it only where you can keep it contained. Purple star flowers with reflexed petals and bright yellow cones give way to shiny red berries that are toxic to people and pets.

planting.note: Plant after the last frost when the ground has thoroughly warmed.

directions: Set plants or seed against a fence or pergola and keep the fast tendrils loosely tied until they catch hold. Water regularly the first season, then let it run lean since it flowers best under some drought stress. This white-flowered form carries the same airy sprays as the species, blooming from summer into fall.

planting.note: Transplant rooted starts after frost when soil is warm.

directions: Plant a rooted start at the base of a strong support and guide the first shoots onto it. This selection carries deeper carmine-red blooms than the ordinary pink form, so give it full sun to bring out the color. Prune to the ground after winter dieback in colder zones and enjoy the reddish sprays through the heat of summer.

planting.note: Sow in warm soil once the frost season has fully ended.

directions: Sow seed a half inch deep in warm ground and provide a heavy trellis or wall for this vigorous climber. It grows even ranker than common coral vine, so give it room and thin the growth if it overwhelms its support. Deep pink flower sprays appear through the warm months and draw in bees and butterflies.

planting.note: Set out container plants in spring once frost has passed.

directions: Plant a nursery start at the foot of a wall or pergola and let its tendrils and holdfasts climb; a light tie helps it get started. Water through the first summers to establish, then keep it evenly moist for the heaviest flowering. Clusters of trumpet-shaped orange-red flowers with yellow throats open through the warm season, peaking in summer.

planting.note: Plant out in spring after the danger of frost has passed.

directions: Set the plant against a strong support in full sun and guide the young stems until they grip on their own. This selection bears larger mauve to rosy-purple trumpets that age with a golden throat and blooms over a long warm season. Prune after the main flush to keep the vine tidy and encourage repeat bloom.

planting.note: Transplant in spring once nights are reliably mild.

directions: Plant against a fence or arbor in a sunny, sheltered spot and keep the roots moist while it establishes. More restrained than its blood-red cousin, it fits a smaller trellis and rewards close planting with fragrance. Vanilla-scented lavender-purple trumpets that fade toward white appear in flushes through much of the warm season.

planting.note: Plant after frost in a sheltered spot with some shade, where the silver leaf veining shows best.

directions: Plant against a warm, sheltered wall in soil enriched with compost and support the young growth until it grips. It colors most strongly with some shade and protection from harsh sun. The dark leaves are marked with silvery-white veins all season and redden in autumn, the reason it is grown.

planting.note: Plant in spring over a fence or trellis, as this species twines rather than sticking to flat walls.

directions: Set out in spring and provide a fence or shrub to scramble through, since its tendrils lack the adhesive pads of Virginia creeper. Very cold-hardy and drought-tolerant once established, it needs little attention. The compound leaves color red to orange in autumn and dark blue berries follow.

planting.note: Plant in spring on a wall; this variety has smaller leaves and clings more tightly than the type.

directions: Plant against masonry where the dense, small-leaved growth can knit closely to the surface. Water to establish, then this tough form needs only annual trimming to shape. The neat foliage turns fiery red in fall, giving a finer-textured cover than ordinary Virginia creeper.

planting.note: Plant after frost against a wall in a warm position for vigorous self-clinging cover.

directions: Set out in spring against a sunny wall and let the adhesive pads take hold of the surface. Provide steady moisture the first season and prune to keep it in bounds. The glossy compound leaves are bright green in summer and flush red in autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sheltered spot on a wall or into a large tree it can climb.

directions: Plant with support and keep the soil moist while it roots in. It scrambles well through trees and over walls in milder gardens. The three-part leaves take on rich red autumn tones alongside small dark berries.

planting.note: Plant in spring against a wall; young leaves emerge with an attractive purple tint.

directions: Propagated from cuttings to stay true, plant against masonry and let the pads grip. New spring growth is flushed purple, maturing to glossy green, then blazing scarlet in fall. Prune each year to keep it clear of windows and eaves.

planting.note: Plant in spring; this cultivar has small, curly leaves for a fine-textured cover.

directions: Grow from cuttings and plant against a wall where its small crinkled leaves knit densely to the surface. Slightly less vigorous than the species, it suits smaller walls. The dainty foliage turns bright crimson-red in autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring in sun to bring out the gold summer leaf color.

directions: Raised from cuttings, this gold-leaved form colors best with good light, so plant against a sunny wall. The bright yellow-green summer foliage shifts to orange and red in autumn. Water to establish and trim yearly to shape.

planting.note: Plant in spring against a wall; noted for large leaves and strong vigor.

directions: Grown from cuttings, plant against a large wall where its big, glossy three-lobed leaves can spread widely. It is vigorous and quickly covers a surface. Foliage turns rich red in fall; prune to keep it within bounds.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a smaller-leaved selection good for restrained spaces.

directions: Propagate from cuttings and plant against a wall where the smaller leaves give a neat, even cover. Less rampant than the species, it fits smaller gardens. The foliage flushes bright red and purple in autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun to deepen the purple leaf tones.

directions: Grown from cuttings, plant against a sunny wall where the foliage holds a purple-red cast through the season. It clings by adhesive pads like the species. Autumn brings even deeper red-purple color before leaf drop.

planting.note: Plant in spring with some afternoon shade to protect the variegated leaves from scorch.

directions: Raised from cuttings, this variegated selection has green leaves marbled creamy white, so give it a spot sheltered from harsh midday sun. Less vigorous than the plain species, it clings to walls by adhesive pads. The white markings blush pink in cool weather and the leaves turn rose-red in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring on a large wall for a golden-yellow autumn display.

directions: Grown from cuttings, plant against a broad surface it can cover by its adhesive tendrils. Unlike the usual red-fall forms, this selection turns clear yellow and gold in autumn. Water to establish and prune yearly to control its reach.

planting.note: Plant in spring on a wall for an intense scarlet autumn show.

directions: Propagated from cuttings, plant against masonry where the vigorous growth can knit tightly by its pads. Selected for reliable, brilliant red autumn color. Give it room and trim each year to keep growth off windows and gutters.

planting.note: Plant in spring on masonry; this variety clings especially tightly to flat surfaces.

directions: Set out in spring against a wall where its strongly adhesive pads make it self-supporting without wires. Water through the first season to establish deep roots. The five-leaflet foliage turns a striking red in fall.

planting.note: Space rooted plants in spring for fast groundcover coverage.

directions: Set plants slightly deeper than the pot line and water well to settle the vigorous root system. This robust ivy spreads rapidly as a groundcover or climber, so trim runners each spring to keep it in bounds. Broad matte-green leaves stay glossy year round and adult shoots flower in fall.

planting.note: Plant after the last spring frost in mild-winter regions.

directions: Set rooted cuttings at the pot depth and water regularly while they knit into a cover. The large glossy leaves and red-tinted stems make a bold warm-climate groundcover or wall vine. It is less cold-hardy than English ivy, so give a sheltered site where winters dip below the low twenties.

planting.note: Plant in spring in frost-free or nearly frost-free gardens.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings at their original depth and keep the soil consistently moist during establishment. This tender ivy carries broad shining leaves and wine-red young stems, thriving in coastal and Mediterranean climates. Protect it from hard frost, which browns the foliage; adult shoots flower in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sheltered, part-shaded spot.

directions: Set rooted plants at the pot line and water in well. This Himalayan species has narrow, often diamond-shaped leaves with a leathery feel and clings to bark and stone. Adult growth carries orange to yellow berries after autumn flowering, unusual among ivies.

planting.note: Plant in spring in humus-rich soil with some shade.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings at their original depth and keep evenly moist while rooting out. This restrained species bears small, tidy rhombic leaves and climbs modestly compared with English ivy. It suits smaller walls and shaded beds, flowering in autumn on mature wood.

planting.note: Plant in spring where winters stay mild.

directions: Set rooted plants at the pot depth and water consistently as they establish. The matte, softly hairy leaves have blunt lobes and a distinctive light green cast. Give this frost-sensitive island species a protected, part-shaded position.

planting.note: Plant in spring in frost-free coastal gardens.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings at their original depth and keep the root zone moist during establishment. This tender ivy from Madeira has broad, faintly lobed leaves and prefers humid, sheltered sites. Shield it from frost and drying wind for best evergreen cover.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, well-drained soil.

directions: Set plants at the pot line and water well to help the roots take hold. This species has thin, glossy, undivided leaves that lie flat against surfaces as it climbs. It tolerates cold better than most large-leaved ivies and flowers in autumn on adult growth.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, sheltered position.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings at their original depth and keep evenly moist as they settle. The leaves show pale veins against a darker green, giving a marbled look prized by collectors. Provide a mild, protected site since this Mediterranean species dislikes hard freezes.

planting.note: Plant in spring in mild-winter gardens.

directions: Set rooted plants at the pot depth and water in thoroughly. This vigorous ivy has large, softly lobed leaves and reddish young stems, making a quick cover in warm regions. Protect from frost and give room to spread across walls or banks.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring; this cultivar is grown only from cuttings.

directions: Set rooted cuttings at their original depth and keep lightly moist while they establish. The silvery-grey leaves edged in cream light up shady corners and containers. Give it part shade to preserve the variegation and pinch stems to keep growth bushy.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in a bright but sheltered spot.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings at the pot line and water gently to settle them. Grey-green leaves with wide golden-yellow margins make this a favorite for edging and pots. Bright light deepens the gold, but shield it from harsh midday sun that can scorch the pale edges.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring against a wall or trellis.

directions: Set rooted cuttings at their original depth and train the pink-tinged young stems onto a support. Each dark green leaf carries a bold splash of yellow in the center, brightest in good light. This vigorous climber, long sold as Goldheart, clothes walls quickly once established.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring where they get bright, filtered light.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings at the depth they grew and keep the soil evenly moist. New leaves emerge butter-yellow in sun, aging to soft green in shade, so site it in bright light for the strongest color. It is one of the finest yellow-leaved ivies for walls and containers.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in containers or as edging.

directions: Set the small rooted cuttings at their original depth and water gently. Named for its little webbed, duck-foot leaves, this compact ivy trails neatly from pots and hanging baskets. Pinch tips to encourage dense, bushy growth in shade or part sun.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in pots or shaded beds.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings at the pot line and keep lightly moist while rooting out. The glossy leaves have curled, ruffled edges that give a lacy, formal look, prized for topiary and containers. Slow and self-branching, it needs little pruning to stay tidy.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring on a support or as groundcover.

directions: Set rooted cuttings at their original depth and water in well. The small leaves have slender, sharply pointed lobes that create a fine, delicate texture. It trails or climbs gracefully and suits both baskets and shaded walls.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring for hardy groundcover in cold regions.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings at the pot depth and water well to establish the spreading roots. Selected for cold hardiness, this ivy has small dark leaves with pale veins and tolerates harsher winters than the plain species. Use it as durable groundcover or a wall climber in northern gardens.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring as tough groundcover.

directions: Set rooted cuttings at their original depth and keep moist through the first season. This hardy selection has large, leathery leaves with prominent white veins and shrugs off cold winters. It makes reliable evergreen groundcover under trees and on shaded slopes.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in pots or shaded ground.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings at the pot line and water gently to settle them. The bright green leaves have jagged, pointed lobes and wavy, rippled edges with pale veining. It climbs or trails with a lively texture and holds color well in shade.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in containers or shade beds.

directions: Set rooted cuttings at their original depth and keep evenly moist while establishing. The star-shaped leaves have wavy, crested lobes that curl downward, taking on bronze and pink tints in cold weather. This self-branching ivy makes a graceful trailer for baskets.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring indoors or out.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings at the pot depth and water well to start them growing. A vigorous, freely branching ivy with medium green five-lobed leaves, long popular both as a houseplant and outdoor climber. Its dense self-branching habit makes it easy to shape and quick to cover.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in pots or as edging.

directions: Set the small rooted cuttings at their original depth and keep lightly moist. The clustered, three-lobed dark green leaves resemble little shamrocks and turn deep bronze in cold weather. This compact, self-branching ivy is ideal for containers and topiary.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in bright, sheltered spots or pots.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings at the pot line and water gently to settle them. The small leaves are heavily splashed and streaked with creamy white on green, giving a bright speckled effect. Grow in good light to keep the variegation strong; it excels in containers and as a houseplant.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in a sheltered, bright position.

directions: Set rooted cuttings at their original depth and keep lightly moist. The small grey-green leaves are edged in creamy white, taking on pink tones in cold weather. This tidy, self-branching ivy is popular for pots, baskets, and as a houseplant; shelter it from hard frost.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in pots or as edging.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings at the pot line and water gently to establish. The small diamond-shaped leaves are grey-green with neat creamy margins and often flush pink in winter. Its compact, bushy habit suits containers, edging, and small topiary forms.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in pots or shaded beds.

directions: Set rooted cuttings at their original depth and keep evenly moist. The rounded bright green leaves have crinkled, frilled edges like parsley, blushing bronze-red in cold spells. This crested ivy trails or climbs and adds ruffled texture to baskets and walls.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in rock gardens or troughs.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings at the pot depth and water gently to settle them. Unlike typical ivy, this non-climbing form makes stiff, upright little stems with neatly ranked triangular leaves. Its slow, congested habit suits rock gardens, troughs, and containers.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring as a specimen or in containers.

directions: Set rooted cuttings at their original depth and keep lightly moist. This upright, non-clinging ivy holds stiff vertical stems lined with tidy triangular leaves, needing no support. Its architectural form makes a striking container or rock-garden accent.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in troughs or small pots.

directions: Plant the tiny rooted cuttings at their original depth and water carefully. This is one of the smallest-leaved ivies, forming a dense mat of miniature dark leaves that redden in winter. Its slow growth makes it ideal for troughs, fairy gardens, and detailed topiary.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in bright shade or containers.

directions: Set rooted cuttings at the pot depth and keep evenly moist while establishing. The rippled, pointed-lobed leaves are green with irregular yellow to cream margins that brighten in good light. It trails or climbs and adds cheerful color to shaded corners.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in a bright, sheltered position.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings at the pot line and water gently to settle them. The light green leaves carry creamy variegation concentrated toward the edges, giving a soft, luminous look. Grow in good light to keep the pale coloring bright in pots and baskets.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring against a wall or fence.

directions: Set rooted cuttings at their original depth and train the shoots onto a support. The large heart-shaped leaves show a central splash of yellow-green that spreads irregularly toward the edges. Also sold as Paddy's Pride, this vigorous ivy quickly brightens shaded walls and fences.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in moist, fertile soil.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings at the pot depth and mulch to keep the roots cool and moist. The very large, softly drooping leaves are grey-green with broad, creamy-yellow margins. This handsome ivy makes a bold cover for walls and shaded banks and grows fast once settled.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in a sheltered, mild-winter spot.

directions: Set rooted cuttings at their original depth and guide the reddish young stems onto a support. The large leaves are deep green in the center, shading through grey-green to broad creamy-white margins. Give this variegated Algerian ivy a protected wall, as it is less cold-tolerant than English ivy.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring for fast cover on large walls.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings at the pot depth and water well to launch the vigorous growth. This robust Algerian selection bears very large, glossy dark green leaves and purple-tinted stems. It quickly clothes big walls and banks in mild climates; shelter it from severe frost.

planting.note: Set out young plants in spring once hard frost has passed and the soil has warmed.

directions: Plant at the base of a sturdy trellis, arbor, or porch post with the crown level with the soil and keep the roots consistently moist. Twine the fast-growing stems onto their support early in the season, since the huge kidney-shaped leaves create dense summer shade. Look for the curved pipe-shaped brownish-purple flowers half-hidden among the foliage in late spring to early summer.

planting.note: Move outdoors only after nights stay reliably warm, or grow in a container to overwinter.

directions: Train onto a trellis or fence in a warm, sheltered spot and keep it evenly watered through the growing season. In cool climates grow it as a container plant and shelter it indoors before frost. The heart-shaped, maroon-and-white marbled flowers that give it its name open through summer and into fall.

planting.note: Plant in late spring in a frost-free, sheltered location with rich soil.

directions: Provide a strong arbor or pergola and feed regularly, as this vine puts on rapid, heavy growth in warmth and humidity. Keep the soil moist but well drained and protect it entirely from frost. The enormous heart-shaped, wine-red flowers veined with cream can reach the size of a dinner plate and appear from summer into autumn.

planting.note: Set out in late spring where nights are warm and frost never occurs.

directions: Grow on a robust support in a warm, humid, frost-free position with rich, moisture-retentive soil. This species produces some of the largest flowers in the genus, a mottled cream-and-maroon bloom trailing a long slender tail. The flowers smell strongly unpleasant for a day as they attract pollinating flies, so site it away from seating areas.

planting.note: Plant out in spring after frost in a warm border or the front of a bed.

directions: This low, spreading species stays small and works well tumbling over a low wall or scrambling through a border rather than climbing high. The rounded leaves are attractively veined in silvery white, and the small yellow flowers have a fringed, dark-throated mouth. It self-sows in mild gardens and serves as a compact larval host for swallowtail butterflies.

planting.note: Plant in late spring in a warm, frost-free garden or a heated greenhouse.

directions: Give this Brazilian climber a warm, humid situation with a sturdy support and steady moisture at the roots. The curved, pouch-like flowers are patterned in reddish brown over a paler ground and flare into a broad lower lip. Blooms come through the warm months and are best grown under glass outside the frost-free tropics.
